content.text: GenAI ideation workshops translate the abstract promise of AI into a concrete, prioritised backlog of use cases grounded in a specific value stream. Without a structured workshop, organisations produce either unfocused wishlists (every function wants a chatbot) or engineering-driven solutions in search of problems (deploying models because the technology is available). A well-run workshop anchors ideation in the actual steps of the value stream, applies the four GenAI impact patterns as generative lenses, and produces a ranked list of candidates ready for discovery scoping.

attrs.alt: GenAI ideation workshop session flow: Frame → Generate → Cluster → Score → Prioritise

content.text: Why Standard Innovation Workshops Fail for GenAI
Traditional innovation workshops (design sprints, hackathons, blue-sky brainstorms) generate ideas that feel inspiring in the room but fail at the technical boundary. The most common failure modes:
Ideas that require data that does not exist

— 'Use AI to predict churn' is popular; discovering that no structured retention data has been captured in 10 years is the reality. GenAI workshops must include a data readiness screen at the ideation stage.
Pattern blindness
— Without explicit GenAI patterns (Augment, Automate, Transform, Insight), participants default to 'build a chatbot'. Most high-value enterprise use cases are not chatbots.
No value stream anchor
— Ideas untethered from a specific value stream step cannot be evaluated for feasibility or sized for business impact. 'Improve customer experience with AI' is not a use case.
Missing technical participants

— Workshops attended only by business stakeholders generate ideas with no feasibility signal. Workshops attended only by engineers generate technically feasible ideas with no business priority signal. Both groups must be in the room.
A GenAI ideation workshop is not a creative free-for-all. It is a structured process that uses the value stream map as a scaffold and the four AI patterns as generative lenses.
Pre-Workshop Preparation
Three artefacts must exist before the workshop date:
Artefact
Owner
Why It Matters
Value stream map (swim lane, with cycle times per step)
Process owner / BU analyst
Provides the scaffold for anchoring ideas — participants ideate per step, not in the abstract
AI pattern primer (1-pager with 4 patterns + one example each)
CoE facilitator
Without prior exposure to the 4 patterns, participants default to chatbots and search
Data landscape summary (what structured/unstructured data exists per step)
Data platform lead
Grounds the feasibility screen — ideas requiring unavailable data are flagged in real time

The workshop facilitator (from the CoE or a trained internal design-thinking practitioner) sends the value stream map and AI pattern primer to participants 48 hours before the session. Asking participants to arrive having read one GenAI case study from their industry shifts the quality of ideas generated.
Participant Selection
Role
Count
Why They Are There
Business process owners (value stream step owners)
Know the pain points and error rates; bring the business impact signal
Front-line practitioners (the people doing the work today)
Know the actual failure modes, workarounds, and exception cases that managers cannot see
Data platform representative
Provides data readiness signal in real time during Generate and Cluster phases
CoE AI engineer or architect
Provides technical feasibility signal; identifies which ideas hit hard technical blockers
Workshop facilitator
Does not generate ideas; runs the process, tracks time, manages discussion

Optimal group size is 8–12. Below 6, the idea pool is too shallow. Above 15, clustering and scoring become unwieldy. For value streams that span multiple BUs, run parallel workshops per BU and combine outputs in a cross-BU synthesis session.
The Five-Phase Workshop Process
Phase 1: Frame (30 minutes)

The facilitator presents the value stream map step by step. For each step, the process owner confirms the cycle time, error rate, and team size. The CoE engineer adds a brief note on what data exists for that step. The facilitator then presents the four AI patterns with one enterprise example each. The framing phase ends with the facilitator posting the scoping boundaries on the whiteboard: which steps are in scope today and which are explicitly excluded.
Phase 2: Generate (45 minutes)

The core ideation phase. The facilitator presents 'How Might We' (HMW) prompts anchored to each AI pattern. Participants write one idea per sticky note in silence for 20 minutes (divergent thinking must precede convergent discussion — open sharing too early creates anchoring effects). After silent generation, participants share stickies in round-robin fashion, with each person explaining their idea in 30 seconds without critique from the group.

# How Might We prompts anchored to GenAI patterns
# Used as stimulus cards during the Generate phase

HMW_TEMPLATES = {
    "Automate": [
        "How might we remove the human entirely from {step} for the 80% of cases that are routine?",
        "How might we process {step} instantly instead of in {current_time}?",
        "How might we scale {step} to handle 10x volume with the same team?",
    ],
    "Augment": [
        "How might we give each {role} a first draft of {output} so they only need to review?",
        "How might we surface the three most relevant precedents before the {role} makes a decision?",
        "How might we alert the {role} to the single most likely error in a {document} before they submit?",
    ],
    "Insight": [
        "How might we predict {outcome} 30 days before it happens so we can intervene early?",
        "How might we identify which {entity} is most likely to {negative_event} this quarter?",
        "How might we turn {raw_data} into a plain-English recommendation for {role} in real time?",
    ],
    "Transform": [
        "How might we create a {product} that did not exist before AI made it economically viable?",
        "How might we serve {customer_segment} who currently cannot afford our {service}?",
        "How might we compress {multi-step process} into a single AI-assisted interaction?",
    ],
}

def generate_hmw(pattern: str, **context) -> list[str]:
    templates = HMW_TEMPLATES.get(pattern, [])
    return [t.format(**{k: v for k, v in context.items() if "{" + k + "}" in t}) for t in templates]

# Example: generating Augment prompts for claims adjuster step
adjuster_prompts = generate_hmw(
    "Augment",
    role="claims adjuster",
    output="settlement recommendation",
    document="FNOL intake form",
)
for prompt in adjuster_prompts:
    print("HMW:", prompt)

# HMW: How might we give each claims adjuster a first draft of settlement recommendation
#       so they only need to review?
# HMW: How might we surface the three most relevant precedents before the claims adjuster
#       makes a decision?
# HMW: How might we alert the claims adjuster to the single most likely error in a
#       FNOL intake form before they submit?
Phase 3: Cluster (30 minutes)

The facilitator groups stickies by value stream step and AI pattern. Duplicate or near-duplicate ideas are merged with the author's consent. Each cluster is given a working title as a use case candidate. The facilitator assigns an AI pattern label to each cluster — if a cluster is ambiguous (e.g., could be Augment or Automate), the ambiguity is noted as a question for the discovery phase, not resolved by workshop consensus.
Phase 4: Score (30 minutes)
Participants score each clustered use case on two dimensions using dot voting (3 dots each, placed on sticky dots):
Impact
— How much business value does this use case unlock if it works? (Business outcome, not technical capability)
Feasibility
— How realistic is it, given what we know about our data and technical environment today?

The CoE engineer and data platform representative have veto power on feasibility scores that are clearly unrealistic (e.g., a use case that requires labelled data that demonstrably does not exist cannot receive a High feasibility score regardless of participant enthusiasm). After dot voting, the facilitator plots candidates on a 2x2 impact/feasibility matrix and reads out the quadrants to the group.
Phase 5: Prioritise (15 minutes)

The group agrees on the top 5–10 use cases to carry forward to the discovery backlog. For each, a business owner and a technical owner are named. The facilitator captures the output in the standard use case intake form and commits to sharing it within 24 hours. Use cases from the other quadrants are not discarded — they are stored in the 'parking lot' for future workshop cycles.
Scoring and Ranking Model

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal

ImpactPattern = Literal["Augment", "Automate", "Transform", "Insight"]
RiskLevel = Literal["Low", "Medium", "High"]

@dataclass
class UseCase:
    title: str
    value_stream: str
    step: str
    pattern: ImpactPattern
    # Impact dimensions (1-5)
    business_impact: int   # Revenue, cost, or NPS effect
    strategic_fit: int     # Alignment with enterprise GenAI strategy
    # Feasibility dimensions (1-5)
    data_readiness: int    # Is clean, labelled data available?
    tech_complexity: int   # 5 = low complexity; 1 = very complex
    change_complexity: int # 5 = low change mgmt burden; 1 = high
    risk_level: RiskLevel = "Medium"
    notes: str = ""

    @property
    def impact_score(self) -> float:
        return (self.business_impact * 0.6 + self.strategic_fit * 0.4)

    @property
    def feasibility_score(self) -> float:
        return (self.data_readiness * 0.4 + self.tech_complexity * 0.3 + self.change_complexity * 0.3)

    @property
    def composite(self) -> float:
        risk_penalty = {"Low": 0, "Medium": 0.3, "High": 0.8}[self.risk_level]
        return round((self.impact_score + self.feasibility_score) / 2 - risk_penalty, 2)

    @property
    def quadrant(self) -> str:
        hi = self.impact_score >= 3.0
        hf = self.feasibility_score >= 3.0
        if hi and hf:  return "Quick Win"
        if hi and not hf: return "Strategic Bet"
        if not hi and hf: return "Low-Hanging Fruit"
        return "Deprioritise"

def rank_use_cases(cases: list[UseCase]) -> list[UseCase]:
    return sorted(cases, key=lambda c: c.composite, reverse=True)

# Workshop output for an insurance claims value stream
candidates = [
    UseCase("FNOL auto-triage", "Claims", "FNOL intake", "Automate",
            business_impact=5, strategic_fit=4,
            data_readiness=4, tech_complexity=4, change_complexity=3,
            risk_level="Low"),
    UseCase("Settlement amount recommendation", "Claims", "Adjuster review", "Augment",
            business_impact=4, strategic_fit=5,
            data_readiness=3, tech_complexity=3, change_complexity=2,
            risk_level="Medium"),
    UseCase("Fraud propensity scoring", "Claims", "Fraud investigation", "Insight",
            business_impact=5, strategic_fit=5,
            data_readiness=3, tech_complexity=2, change_complexity=4,
            risk_level="Medium"),
    UseCase("Customer-facing claims chatbot", "Claims", "Customer comms", "Automate",
            business_impact=3, strategic_fit=3,
            data_readiness=4, tech_complexity=3, change_complexity=2,
            risk_level="High"),
    UseCase("Policy document summarisation", "Underwriting", "Policy review", "Augment",
            business_impact=3, strategic_fit=3,
            data_readiness=5, tech_complexity=5, change_complexity=5,
            risk_level="Low"),
]

ranked = rank_use_cases(candidates)
print(f"{'Rank':<5} {'Title':<40} {'Quadrant':<18} {'Composite':>9}")
print("-" * 75)
for i, uc in enumerate(ranked, 1):
    print(f"{i:<5} {uc.title:<40} {uc.quadrant:<18} {uc.composite:>9.2f}")

# Output:
# Rank  Title                                    Quadrant           Composite
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1     FNOL auto-triage                         Quick Win               4.10
# 2     Policy document summarisation            Quick Win               4.00
# 3     Fraud propensity scoring                 Quick Win               3.55
# 4     Settlement amount recommendation         Strategic Bet           3.25
# 5     Customer-facing claims chatbot           Low-Hanging Fruit       2.95
The Impact-Feasibility Matrix
The 2×2 matrix produced by the scoring phase has four quadrants. Each quadrant implies a different next action:
Quadrant
Impact
Feasibility
Description
Next Step
Quick Win
High
High
High value, achievable now. The organisation should commit to delivery.
Enter discovery sprint within 30 days
Strategic Bet
High
Low
High value but blocked by data, regulation, or technology gaps. Worth investing to unblock.
Plan capability gap closure; revisit in 6–12 months
Low-Hanging Fruit
Low
High
Easy to build but limited business impact. Useful for team learning; do not anchor the programme on these.
Implement as team capability-building; not programme priorities
Deprioritise
Low
Low
Neither high impact nor feasible. Archive.
Park; do not invest further unless context changes

The most important conversation in Phase 4 is about Strategic Bets. A High-Impact/Low-Feasibility idea reveals where the organisation must invest in data infrastructure, change management, or regulatory approval to unlock future AI value. This is as strategically useful as finding Quick Wins.
Workshop Output: The Use Case Intake Form

Every use case that exits Phase 5 into the discovery backlog must have a completed intake form. The form prevents half-formed ideas from entering discovery and wasting engineering time. Required fields:
Field
Example
Use case title
FNOL auto-triage classification
Value stream
Insurance claims — FNOL to settlement
Target step
FNOL intake
AI pattern
Automate
Problem statement
18% of FNOL submissions have data errors requiring manual correction, adding 2 days to cycle time
Proposed AI intervention
Multi-label classifier trained on 5 years of historical FNOL submissions to identify missing/invalid fields at intake and auto-route to the correct claims team
Expected business outcome
Reduce FNOL error rate from 18% to <5%; reduce intake cycle time from 3 days to same-day
Data available?
Yes — 50k labelled historical FNOLs in CMS; requires enrichment with outcome labels
Known risks
Class imbalance for rare claim types; regulatory requirement to provide human review option
Business owner
Head of Claims Operations
Technical owner
CoE ML Engineer
Common Facilitation Anti-Patterns
Generating ideas first, framing second
— Without the value stream map and AI pattern primer in place before Generate begins, participants produce generic ideas. Always run Phase 1 in full before opening the sticky note pads.
Allowing HiPPO scoring

— The Highest-Paid Person's Opinion should carry no special weight in dot voting. The facilitator must frame scoring as anonymous and individual — participants place dots simultaneously, not sequentially.
Skipping the data readiness check

— Ideas are exciting in the abstract. The data platform representative's role is to inject reality. Do not let them be passive. Build in a structured 'data challenge' moment for each top-ten candidate.
Over-scheduling
— A 2.5-hour workshop produces better output than a 6-hour workshop with the same objectives. Divergent thinking degrades sharply after 90 minutes without a substantial break.
No owner assigned before participants leave
— Ideas with no named owner are dead on arrival. The final 5 minutes of Phase 5 must produce a name next to each use case — no exceptions.
Scaling to Multiple Value Streams

An enterprise GenAI programme typically runs ideation workshops across 4–8 value streams in the first quarter. Running them sequentially takes too long; running them all in parallel produces inconsistent outputs that cannot be compared for cross-enterprise prioritisation. The recommended cadence:
Run workshops for the 2–3 highest-priority value streams (as identified by the executive sponsor) in weeks 1–3.
Synthesise outputs into a cross-enterprise priority matrix in week 4 — the CoE Lead chairs this session with the programme sponsor.
Run the next tier of value stream workshops in weeks 5–8, feeding into the same cross-enterprise priority matrix.
Hold a quarterly review at the end of each quarter to re-rank the combined backlog against actual delivery progress.

The cross-enterprise priority matrix uses the same scoring dimensions (impact and feasibility) but adds two additional columns: strategic alignment (how closely does this use case align to the enterprise GenAI strategy pillars?) and dependency (does this use case depend on platform capabilities that are not yet built?). Use cases blocked by platform dependencies are tracked in the platform roadmap, not the use case backlog.
Integration with the Use Case Governance Gate

A use case ideation workshop does not approve anything for development. It produces candidates for the discovery backlog. Each candidate must pass through the use case governance gate — a structured review by the CoE Lead, the business sponsor, and the programme PMO — before engineering resources are allocated. The governance gate checks four things:
Gate Criterion
Checked By
Pass Condition
Business case exists
Programme PMO
Business outcome, baseline, and target are defined and signed off by the BU owner
Data readiness confirmed
Data platform lead
At least one viable data source identified; data access approved or in progress
AI pattern correctly assigned
CoE Lead
Pattern (Augment/Automate/Transform/Insight) is consistent with the intended intervention; not retrofitted
Risk pre-screen passed
CoE Risk lead
No immediate regulatory, privacy, or model risk red flags that would block delivery before investment is made

Use cases that fail the governance gate are returned to the parking lot with a written note explaining the gap — not discarded. The note becomes the input for the capability gap closure plan (data procurement, regulatory engagement, model risk pre-clearance) that would enable the use case to re-enter the backlog in a later cycle.
Governance Gate Timing
Workshop output (use case intake forms) submitted to CoE within 24 hours of workshop close.
CoE Lead and data platform lead complete intake review within 5 business days.
Governance gate review meeting held within 10 business days of workshop.
Approved use cases enter the discovery sprint queue; rejected use cases receive written feedback within 3 business days.
Worked Example: Full Workshop Walkthrough
The following table traces a single use case — 'FNOL auto-triage' — from sticky note to scored backlog entry, illustrating what happens at each workshop phase:
Phase
What Happened
Output
Frame

Facilitator walked through the claims FNOL step: 3-day average intake cycle time, 18% error rate, 4 FTE handling intake manually. AI pattern primer introduced Automate pattern with example from e-commerce order routing.
Team understood the value stream bottleneck and knew what Automate meant before generating ideas
Generate (silent)
Three participants independently wrote 'auto-classify incoming claim types' on stickies. Two wrote 'auto-route to correct claims team'. One wrote 'flag missing fields at intake'.
6 distinct stickies in the Automate/FNOL cluster
Cluster
Facilitator merged the 6 stickies into one cluster: 'FNOL intake auto-triage and routing'. Labelled Automate pattern. Data rep noted: '50k historical FNOLs available in CMS with team routing labels'.
One named use case candidate: 'FNOL auto-triage' — data confirmed available
Score

Impact: 4 dots from business owners (high cycle time impact), 2 from technical (manageable). Feasibility: 5 dots from technical (data available, standard classification task), 3 from business (worried about accuracy for complex claims).
High Impact / High Feasibility → Quick Win quadrant
Prioritise
Ranked #1 of 9 candidates by composite score (4.10). Assigned to Head of Claims Ops (business) and CoE ML Engineer (technical). Intake form completed before participants left the room.
Ready for discovery sprint within 30 days

The key moment in this walkthrough is the data representative's confirmation during Cluster that labelled historical data exists. Without that confirmation, the same use case would have landed in the Strategic Bet quadrant (High Impact / Low Feasibility) and would not have been prioritised for the next discovery sprint.
Remote and Hybrid Workshop Adaptations
In distributed organisations, the workshop cannot always be run in person. The five-phase structure transfers to virtual formats with specific tool adaptations:
Phase
In-Person Tool
Virtual Equivalent
Frame
Printed value stream map on wall
Shared Miro/FigJam board with digital swim lanes; participants annotate in real time
Generate
Physical sticky notes on butcher paper
Digital sticky notes on virtual board; same silent 20-min timer; breakout rooms for subgroups
Cluster
Facilitator physically moves stickies
Facilitator groups digital stickies; all participants can see moves; clustering vote in shared view
Score
Dot stickers placed on sticky clusters
Anonymous emoji or star votes per card; use polling tool for simultaneous reveal
Prioritise
Facilitator writes owner names on flipchart
Shared backlog sheet updated live; screen-shared; exported to intake form template immediately
Virtual workshops run 20% longer than in-person equivalents for the same output. Plan for 3 hours with two 10-minute breaks rather than 2.5 hours.

Silent generation is easier to enforce virtually (cameras off, no one can see others writing). This often produces more diverse ideas than in-person sessions where participants self-censor in visible proximity to senior colleagues.
The Cluster phase is the hardest to facilitate virtually. Use a co-facilitator to manage the chat while the lead facilitator moves cards — one person cannot do both effectively.
Summary

GenAI ideation workshops require a value stream map, an AI pattern primer, and a data landscape summary before the session starts. Without these anchors, workshops produce ideas that cannot be evaluated or implemented.

The five-phase structure — Frame, Generate, Cluster, Score, Prioritise — separates divergent and convergent thinking. Silent sticky generation before open sharing prevents anchoring and HiPPO dominance.
HMW prompts anchored to the four GenAI patterns (Augment, Automate, Transform, Insight) are the primary generative stimulus. Pattern-free ideation defaults to chatbot suggestions.
The scoring model (Impact × Feasibility) with a risk penalty produces a composite rank. The CoE engineer and data platform representative hold veto power on infeasible feasibility scores.

Every use case entering the discovery backlog requires a completed intake form with a named business owner, technical owner, problem statement, proposed intervention, expected outcome, data availability assessment, and known risks.
At scale, run parallel workshops across value streams with a quarterly cross-enterprise synthesis session to maintain a prioritised, unified backlog.
